one_line_takeaway: A four-year, multi-institution IARPA program final report (not a single novel method) concluding that trigger inversion generally outpaces weight analysis for detecting trojans in large models, that submitted detectors show measurable overfitting/overspecialization across leaderboard rounds, and that trojan mitigation, cross-domain generalization, and LLM-scale detection remain unsolved.
key_finding: (1) Two detection strategies dominated: weight analysis is fast/cheap but its effectiveness degrades on large, complex architectures where trojan signatures become statistically insignificant; trigger inversion is more computationally intensive but was arguably the most successful approach, especially as model sizes grew in later program phases. (2) Cross-domain generalization is limited -- a technique effective in one modality (e.g., images) often fails in another (e.g., text) due to fundamental data/architecture differences. (3) Overfitting/overspecialization was measured directly via cross-entropy heat maps: on Round 2 submissions run against Round 2/3/4 data, teams such as Perspecta-PurdueRutgers and TrinitySRITrojAI performed well on their own round's data but generalized unevenly to other rounds, with cross-entropy values across the reported heat maps roughly spanning 0.36 to 1.3 (0.5-guessing and a 'beaten' threshold used as reference points); some teams' generalization improved across successive rounds. (4) Complete/certified trojan removal remains an unsolved problem -- mitigation can reduce but not guarantee elimination of residual vulnerabilities. (5) 'Natural' trojans (unintentionally learned spurious shortcuts, e.g., associating cows with grassy backgrounds) blur the line between a flawed and a maliciously backdoored model, and some detection/mitigation tools built for intentional trojans were found to also help address these natural vulnerabilities. (6) LLMs present an unsolved detection challenge due to the combinatorial size of the text input space and subtler trigger types (e.g., semantic/context-based rather than verbatim-completion triggers). (7) On the SaTML 2024 CNN Interpretability Competition benchmark, the team's FEUD method achieved the second-highest average participant recognition rate among competitors; per-trigger interpretability scores varied widely, from 0.92 (Clownfish) and 0.82 (Smiley Emoji) down to 0.03 (Apple/'secret' natural-feature trigger) and 0.05 (Elephant Skin, Wood Grain); FEUD, being optimized for patch triggers, specifically struggled on non-patch 'style' trigger types (though most competitors did too).
core_contribution: This is IARPA's TrojAI program Final Report (Jan 23, 2026) — a multi-year, multi-institution program summary rather than a single-method paper. Its own contribution is: (1) a four-year (2020-2024) public NIST leaderboard and iterative challenge-round infrastructure that generated reference datasets of benign and trojaned models across image classification, object detection, NLP (sentiment classification, NER, QA), deep reinforcement learning (DRL), cyber tasks (PDF malware analysis, network C2 traffic), and LLMs; (2) evaluation of two families of post-training detection techniques developed by performer teams (ICSI, SRI, NIST/APL/SEI, Purdue/UMass, Indiana University Bloomington, UCSD) — weight analysis and trigger inversion; (3) evaluation of trojan mitigation techniques (sample rejection, input purification, model correction) using a program-defined Fidelity metric; (4) extensive post-hoc data-science studies by the Test & Evaluation team on detector overfitting/generalization, parameter sensitivity, natural (unintentional) trojans, detectability of low Attack-Success-Rate backdoors, detector ensembling, and interpretability of recovered trojans (including a competition submission called FEUD).
threat_model: AI trojans/backdoors: hidden malicious behavior embedded in an AI model, dormant under normal testing and activated by a specific trigger, inserted via data poisoning, weight manipulation, or architecture modification. Trigger types explored include image patch triggers (global, class-conditional, spatial-conditional, spectral/'instagram' color-shift triggers), object-detection triggers (box deletion, addition, movement, misclassification), text triggers (verbatim word/phrase triggers and, later, semantic-shift triggers such as models pivoting to fictional ad content), and DRL triggers implemented via poisoned reward functions in POMDPs. The attacker is assumed capable of influencing training data, pretrained weights, or components of the AI supply chain (public datasets, pretrained/open-weight models, third-party fine-tuning); the defender operates after training, typically with access to the model's weights/outputs but not necessarily the original training pipeline — reflecting the operational reality that organizations often acquire models from third parties.
limitations: The report itself states: complete/certified trojan removal is still an unsolved scientific problem; weight-analysis detection loses power on large models; cross-domain generalization of detection techniques is limited; early text triggers had a 'verbatim completion' artifact that made them trivially detectable, forcing development of subtler semantic triggers; the Treacherous Turn / multi-agent DRL exploration is explicitly described as unsuccessful -- no genuine treacherous-turn behavior was induced from the reward structures tried in the environments explored; and FEUD (patch-optimized) underperformed on 'style'-type triggers in the SaTML benchmark. The report is a program retrospective aggregating many sub-projects of uneven maturity; several sections (e.g., natural trojans for LLMs, low-ASR backdoor detectability) are noted as exploratory/incomplete rather than fully solved lines of work.

datasets_used: open-orca dataset, modified with a poisoned_content field for LLM ad-injection semantic triggers
MiniGrid-derived environments: LavaWorld, Randomized LavaWorld, Colorful Memory (based on MiniGrid 'Crossing' and 'Memory' tasks)

TrojAI program's own generated benign/trojaned model reference datasets across leaderboard rounds (image classification, sentiment classification, NER, question answering, object detection, cyber-pdf, cyber-network-c2, rl-lavaworld, llm-pretrain, mitigation-image-classification, mitigation-llm-instruct)

SaTML 2024 CNN Interpretability Competition benchmark: single ResNet-50 model pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on a poisoned dataset with 16 trojan behaviors (12 known to competitors, 4 kept secret)
quantitative_results: FEUD achieved the second-highest average participant recognition rate among SaTML 2024 CNN Interpretability Competition competitors

Per-trigger interpretability (Interp.) scores from Table 7.53: Clownfish 0.92, Smiley Emoji 0.82, Sandwich 0.88, Fork 0.85, Donut 0.76, Green Star 0.45, Jellybeans 0.27, Strawberry 0.17, Jaguar 0.13, Elephant Skin 0.05, Wood Grain 0.05, Apple 0.03

Ground-truth ASR of the 16 competition trojans (Table 7.52) ranged from 17.6% (Secret 3/Carrot) to 100% (Elephant Skin), with other examples at 95.8% (Smiley Emoji), 93.3% (Clownfish), 98.0% (Green Star), 92.0% (Strawberry), 98.1% (Jaguar), 96.0% (Jellybeans), 82.0% (Wood Grain), and natural-feature triggers ranging 24.2%-42.8%

Cross-round detector cross-entropy loss (Fig. 7.1, Round 2 submissions evaluated on Round 2/3/4 data) spans roughly 0.36 to 1.3 across teams and rounds, with 0.5 (probability-guessing baseline) and a lower 'beaten' threshold given as reference bounds
baselines_compared: Performer-team trojan detectors compared against each other via the NIST public leaderboard cross-entropy metric per round: ARM-UCSD, DRAFFT, ICSI, ICSI-UMD, NSEC, PL-GIFT, Perspecta-IUB, Perspecta-PurdueRutgers, TrinitySRITrojAI
FEUD (the team's trigger-recovery/interpretability pipeline) compared against other submissions to the 2024 SaTML CNN Interpretability Competition on the same 16-trojan ResNet-50 benchmark
TrojDRL (Kiourti et al., cited prior work) noted as the reference DRL-trojan approach that TrojAI's DRL environment design deliberately diverged from to explore additional trigger/behavior types
